specification.topic-statement: The panic handler performs emergency process termination by restoring terminal state, emitting a fatal log entry, writing a diagnostic message to stderr, and aborting the process to generate a core dump.

scope.in-scope: Terminal restoration on panic, logger write on panic, stderr diagnostic output on panic, process termination via abort, and the talloc error handler that delegates to the panic path.
scope.boundary: The panic handler accesses a global terminal context set during startup. If the context is present and has a valid file descriptor, terminal escape sequences are written to that descriptor and terminal attributes are restored.
The panic handler accesses a global logger set during startup. If the logger is present and has a valid file descriptor, a JSON log entry is written to that descriptor.

behavior.panic:
A panic is triggered by providing an error message string, a source file name string, and a line number. Either string may be absent. The following steps execute in order:
If the global terminal context is present and its file descriptor is valid, a reset sequence is written directly to the file descriptor. The sequence shows the cursor (ESC[?25h), resets text attributes (ESC[0m), and exits the alternate screen buffer (ESC[?1049l), as a single 18-byte write. The original terminal attributes are then restored immediately using a direct system call.
The terminal attribute restore call is not async-signal-safe per POSIX. It is used here anyway because it is necessary for the terminal to be usable after the process terminates. The escape sequence write is async-signal-safe.
If the global logger is present and its file descriptor is valid, a JSON object is formatted and written to that descriptor. The object contains: - "level": "fatal" - "event": "panic" - "message": the message string (empty string if absent) - "file": the file string (empty string if absent) - "line": the line number as a decimal integer
The formatted string is followed by a newline. If the formatted length would exceed 511 bytes, nothing is written.
The string formatting function used here is not async-signal-safe. The log write uses a direct write call, which is async-signal-safe, but the formatting is not.
A diagnostic message is written to standard error using only the direct write system call. The format is:
FATAL: <message>\n at <file>:<line>\n
If the message string is absent, that portion is empty. If the file string is absent, that portion is empty. The line number is converted to a decimal string using a custom integer-to-string routine that avoids all non-signal-safe functions.
The integer-to-string conversion negates negative input values to obtain absolute digits. If the line number is exactly the minimum signed 32-bit integer value (-2147483648), negating it produces signed integer overflow, which is undefined behavior. Line numbers in practice are always positive, making this unreachable.
The integer-to-string buffer is 15 characters. Numbers requiring more than 15 digits are silently truncated. This is unreachable for line numbers within any realistic source file.
The process is terminated by calling abort, which raises SIGABRT and generates a core dump if the system is configured to do so.
behavior.talloc-abort-handler: When the memory management library detects an internal error (such as a double free or heap corruption), it calls this handler with a reason string. If the reason string is present, it is passed as the panic message. If the reason string is absent, the message "talloc abort" is used. The file is recorded as "<talloc>" and the line number as zero. The panic sequence then executes as described above.

coverage-exclusion.notable: The entire panic handler is excluded from code coverage measurement. Because every execution path through the handler ends with process termination via abort, no test can observe a return and coverage cannot be collected.
